BAHAMA ISLANDS
Area, etc.
The total land area of the Colony is 4,404 square miles. It is separated on the west from Florida by the Straits of Florida and on the south from Cuba by the Old Bahama and Nicholas Channels.
Population
The population at the end of 1963 was estimated at 131,428. Except for about 20%, the inhabitants are of the negro race. English is universally spoken.
Principal Town
Nassau, the capital, is the chief port and the only town of any size. It is situated on the island of New Providence, the population of which is estimated to be 81,591.
Geographical Features

The Colony consists of an archipelago of about 700 islands, of which only 20 are inhabited and over 2,000 rocks. The islands are generally long, narrow and low-lying and are of aeolian limestone formation. The soil, except in small pockets, is shallow and overlays hard rock. On the whole, it is suitable only for peasant farming. Conditions are sub-tropical and both temperate and tropical trees grow literally side by side. It is probable that at one time all the islands were thickly wooded but little forest now remains except on Abaco, Andros and Grand Bahama, where there are extensive pine forests.
Climate

The winter climate is delightful. Frost is unknown, the average temperature is 70°, the rainfall is slight and cool breezes prevail. In July, August and September the heat ranges from 80° to 90° and though the islands are never unhealthy, the change in temperature throughout the 24 hours is so small that those who remain in the Colony for the whole year may find the long summer somewhat enervating, though this is mitigated in modern days by air conditioning. Although the customarily recognised season when the majority of visitors come to the Bahamas is in the winter, there is a growing number of persons who prefer the summer months because of the opportunities for swimming, sailing and fishing.
The rainfall averages about 40 inches a year. The rainy months are May, June, September and October.
History

San Salvador, one of the islands composing the Colony, and identical with Watling's Island, was the first land discovered by Columbus on his voyage in 1492. A few years later all the Arawak inhabitants were transported to work in the Hispaniola mines. It does not appear that the Spaniards had settlements on any of the islands of the group at any time. In 1649 Parliament, on the petition of William Sayle, a former Governor of Bermuda, and others, passed "An Act for the Adventurers for the Eleutherian Islands" which constituted Sayle and his associates the proprietors of the islands. Notwithstanding an earlier Royal Grant in 1629 and the Cromwellian Act of 1649, Charles II in 1670 granted the islands to six of the Lords Proprietors of Carolina. Before the 1670 Grant the inhabitants of the islands had organized the settlement and instituted a form of government, which included an elective House of Assembly, and had selected their Governor. His appointment was later confirmed by the Lords Proprietors and a regular system of government was established including a parliament, the lower house of which was elective, and this was continued with several breaks until the civil and military government of the islands was resumed by the Crown in 1717.
The settlement on New Providence was sacked by the Spaniards on several occasions between 1680 and 1684. In 1684 all the inhabitants were driven away, and it was not until 1686 that the
Digitized by Google
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

settlement was re-formed by their return, principally from Jamaica. By 1703 it had reached some importance, when it was almost annihilated by the French and Spaniards. However, a year or so after this the dispersed inhabitants returned once more. The islands became a regular rendezvous for pirates and this finally determined the Crown to resume the civil and military government of the place; since 1717 there has been a continuous line of Royal Governors. The islands were surrendered to a fleet of the American rebels in 1776, and again to the Spaniards in 1782, but were re-taken by a British force under Colonel Deveaux and British possession was confirmed by the Peace of Versailles, 1783. After the American Revolution a number of loyalists settled throughout the Bahamas group and received substantial assistance from England, and in 1787 the Lords Proprietors surrendered all their proprietary rights to the King for the sum of £12,000, which was provided by Parliament.

The constitution of the Bahamas was eventually settled in 1729, when the Crown assumed direct control of the colony. The government was modelled upon that of England in the early days, the Governor representing the Sovereign, and the nominated Legislative Council and the elected House of Assembly representing respectively the Houses of Lords and Commons.

Up to January, 1964, representative, but not responsible government existed. The executive government was in the hands of a Governor, appointed by the Crown, who had the power of veto, and was advised by an Executive Council of not more than nine members of whom six were unofficials. Various executive powers and the right to enact certain subsidiary legislation were vested by law in the Governor in Council. A Legislative Council was created as a separate Council by Royal Letters Patent in 1841; and in 1963 it consisted of eleven members nominated by the Crown, of whom nine were unofficials.

The General Assembly Elections Act of 1959 provided for, inter alia , adult male suffrage, and a limited second vote in respect of ownership and/or rental of real property, the latter to be exercised only in a constituency other than that in which the elector resided. The Act also provided for the creation of two additional seats for the Southern and Eastern Districts of New Providence and the redistribution of the New Providence seats after by-elections to fill the new seats. Accordingly from 1960 the House of Assembly was composed of 33 members.
A law was promulgated with effect from mid-1962 extending the franchise to women and making women eligible for election to seats in the House of Assembly.
Constitution and Administration

At the Bahamas Constitutional Conference held in London in May, 1963, the Secretary of State agreed to further constitutional changes. These were embodied in a new Constitution which came into effect on the 7th January 1964, giving the territory full internal self-government.
The bicameral Legislature has been reconstituted to consist of an Upper House called the Senate and a Lower House called the House of Assembly.

The Senate consists of fifteen members of whom eight are appointed by the Governor after consultation with the Premier and such other persons as he may in his discretion decide to consult, five by the Governor on the advice of the Premier, and
two by the Governor on the advice of the Leader of the Opposition.

The House of Assembly remains unchanged until it is dissolved in the ordinary course. Thereafter the House of Assembly will consist of thirty-eight elected members, of whom not less than eighteen nor more than twenty-two members will represent Out Island constituencies, and not less than sixteen nor more than twenty members will represent New Providence constituencies. The next General Election will be contested on the basis of twenty-one seats for the Out Islands and seventeen for New Providence.

The Cabinet will consist of the Premier and not fewer than eight other Ministers. The Governor appoints as Premier the person who appears to him to be best able to command a majority in the House of Assembly. The remaining Ministers are appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier.
Land Policy
The Status of Aliens Act Ch. 180 empowers an alien to acquire and hold property within the Colony.
Taxation

There is no income tax. Excise tax is charged on spirits and beer locally produced at the rate of four-fifths (4/5) of the import duty imposed on the particular kind of spirit or beer. The general Customs duty is 20% ad valorem plus 7½% ad valorem Emergency Tax. Staple foodstuffs pay a low specific duty and no Emergency Tax; spirits and tobacco are taxed at higher rates than the general and gasoline pays a specific duty with additional sales tax. Preferential margins are designed to implement the Ottawa and Anglo-American Trade Agreements.
Public Finance
Revenue Expenditure

The main sources of revenue are Customs dues and receipts from fees, Post Office, Licences and public utilities. In 1963 £5,979,487 of the revenue was derived from import duties. For the same year the main heads of expenditure were :
Development Board (£1,136,813), Medical Department (£1,091,367), Public Works (£1,100,369), Education (£919,944) and Telecommunications (£661,315).
In December 1963 the outstanding amount of the authorized Public Debt was £1,651,771.
Currency

The legal tender currency is British sterling, although United States and Canadian currencies are also generally accepted in the Colony. Currency notes of the denominations of 4s., 10s., £1 and £5 are issued by the Bahamas Government. Accounts are kept in sterling. There are branch banks of the Royal Bank of Canada, Bank of Nova Scotia, Canadian Bank of Commerce, Barclays Bank, D.C. and O., Bank of London and Montreal, First National City Bank of New York and Chase Manhattan Bank in Nassau.

BAHAMAS
Development

The development of a Free Port in the island of Grand Bahama offers excellent prospects for employment in the future. The proposed development, financed by American capital, embraces the construction of a large deep-water harbour at Hawksbill Creek. The Free Port Authority has acquired 50,000 acres of land and plans have been drawn up for zoned areas in the vicinity of the proposed harbour, for industrial projects of all kinds and town planning on the most up-to-date lines. This large-scale project is not a Government scheme but was approved by the Bahamas Legislature. Licences to operate within the Free Port area are granted by the Free Port Authority.

The Bahamas Government has undertaken to supply administrative, police, customs and immigration staff which will remain under its control. The Free Port Authority will provide its own medical and educational facilities but there is provision for inspection of these services by Bahamas Government medical and education officers. In general the closest liaison has been established between the Government and the Authority.
Education

A new comprehensive Education Act known as the Education Act 1962, and modelled in principle on the 1944 Education Act in the United Kingdom, came into effect on 1st September 1962. The new Act placed all government effort in the field of education under one authority, the Board of Education, and provided the machinery for the development of a modern system of education in the future.

The Act was amended in December 1963 in anticipation of the New Constitution which came into effect on 7th January 1964 and which gives the Bahamas internal self-government. All government effort in Education has now become the responsibility of the Minister for Education. The former Board of Education no longer exists but the Minister is advised by the Central Advisory Council for Education.
Under the Act education is compulsory between the ages of 5 and 14. Of a total of 148 Government primary schools, 124 are rural, all-age schools.
There is one reformatory school for boys under the Industrial School Act and one for girls.
No local provision is made for Higher Education and students seeking this type of training must go abroad. Bahamians studying abroad in 1963-64 session were:
124 in the United Kingdom
100 in the United States of America
12 in Canada (as far as can be ascertained)
2 in University of the West Indies
Total 238.
The Teachers' Training College which was temporarily closed in 1957 was re-opened in September 1961. In addition, the Ministry of Education now has a scholarship scheme for:
(a) Refresher courses for experienced teachers;
(b) Degree courses;
(c) Specialist Diploma courses. Most of these courses are tenable in the United Kingdom, a few in Jamaica, West Indies and a few in Canada.

Libraries
There are three Public Libraries in Nassau and 27 in the Out Islands.
Health
The Curative needs of the Colony are met by:

(a) Princess Margaret Hospital with disciplines and full-time Consultant Specialists in Medicine, Surgery, Anaesthesia, Paediatrics, Chest Diseases, Pathology, Radiology, Obstetrics & Gynaecology and Ophthalmology. An O.P.D. with 24-hour Emergency Casualty Service is provided.
(b) The Mental Hospital with Consultant Psychiatrist.
(c) The Geriatric Hospital.
(d) O.I. District Medical Officers (6).
(e) O.I. Clinics (42).

There is also a Health Centre and a Cottage Hospital in Eleuthera and a further Cottage Hospital in Inagua. A District Nursing Service exists. Public Health is administered from Nassau and the Public Health Nursing Service and Schools' Medical and Dental Service cater for all preventive services.
In Nassau there is the Private Rassin Clinic.
Medical care is free to the needy and sub-economic rates are charged others.

In 1963, there were 11,786 admissions and 438 deaths. Maternity cases were 3,550. In the Casualty O.P.D., 41,314 patients were seen. In general the pattern of diseases is subtropical and not tropical, with respiratory diseases of all ages present and Tuberculosis appearing to be capable of control, hypertensive states common and social diseases e.g., Amoebiasis, Enteritis, Venereal diseases and malnutrition all associated with poor housing and utilities common in those areas concerned.

The General Public Health remains satisfactory and the Public Health Nursing Service increased its numbers in Infant Welfare, Immunisation and Vaccination, Home visiting, Ante and post-natal, and Communicable diseases services.

Communications

Nassau International Airport is the main airfield in the Bahamas. It provides 24 hours service for long range international and local airlines. In addition, there are 29 landing strips completed in the various Out Islands. Scheduled international air services were operated into Nassau by B.O.A.C. from London via New York and Bermuda and from Montego Bay, Jamaica; Pan American World Airways operate daily flights from Miami and New York; Trans Canada Airlines from Montreal via Tampa, Florida; Mackey Airlines from Tampa, Fort Lauderdale, Palm Beach, Miami and Jacksonville; Bahamas Airways Limited operates between Nassau and Miami several times daily.
Bahamas Airways Limited operates daily scheduled flights to many of the Out Islands. Bahamas Air Traders operates a fleet of amphibian aircraft on charter to the Out Islands and Florida.
During 1963, 17,566 landings were recorded at Nassau International Airport.

On Eleuthera there is an asphalt main road some 100 miles in length covering the whole of the Island and on Grand Bahama, 65 miles of motor road has been built. Asphalt roads are under construction at Andros, Exuma, Eleuthera, Cat Island, Spanish Wells, San Salvador, Long Island and Crooked Island, where previously only rough tracks existed.
There are no railways in the Colony.

There are regular freight services between the Colony and New York and Florida; Halifax and Montreal (Canadian National Steamship Line); and the United Kingdom (Royal Mail Line and Pacific Steam Navigation Company). West India Steamships Ltd. operate a service between Nassau and Inagua with intermediate stops.
In 1963 6,493 ships entered and 4,180 were cleared at Nassau. Their total tonnage was 10,066,569, of which 1,882,809 tons was British.
Ships also call at Inagua and Pine Ridge to take on salt and lumber exports.
Sound and Television Broadcasting

Government owns, and the Bahamas Broadcasting and Television Commission operates, a broadcasting station located in Nassau. The power of its transmitter is 10 kW., frequency 1,540 kc/s and call sign ZNS. Commercial operation began in 1950, though a broadcasting station has existed since 1936. The Commission took over on 1st January 1957. The broadcast receiver licence fee was abolished in 1954. At that time there were 5,990 licence holders.
Government supplied and maintains 60 community receivers in school-houses in the Out Islands where there may be as many as 200 listeners in the evening.

In August 1962, a second channel opened transmission on a frequency of 1,240 kc/s with a power of 250 watts. The call sign is ZNS II. This covers the island of New Providence only. Commercial advertisements are restricted to single spots between programmes.
Principal Occupations
The tourist traffic continues to be the main industry of the Colony and is the chief source of revenue and of dollars.
The following table shows the number and nationality of visitors to New Providence during the years 1957–63:
Nationality—per cent.
Agricultural work in the United States provides employment for an average of 1,255 unskilled Bahamian labourers and their earnings in 1963 amounted to £769,116.
Main Crops and Products
The main crops are fresh vegetables, tomatoes, okra, pineapples, peas, citrus, bananas, avocados and sisal. Some sugar cane is grown in the Colony.

The Minister is fully alive to the importance of expanding the livestock husbandry of the Colony and of improving the quality of the products. This is being attained by the importation of purebred animals to facilitate the up-grading of local stocks of sheep and pigs; by the provision of pastures for small groups of farmers and by encouraging the establishment of herds of beef and dairy cattle. The estimated livestock population is: sheep 23,000, goats 14,000, swine 10,600, cattle 3,500, horses 3,520, poultry 610,000.

Crawfishing and scale fishing are extensively carried out during the open season (September 1st to March 15th). The bulk of the catch is exported to the United States. The following marine products are also exported: sponges, salt, turtle shell, beach shells and marine curios.

There are four canning plants in the Colony which are engaged principally in canning tomatoes and pineapples. Straw products are manufactured as cottage industries and the raw material for this work is obtained chiefly from palm fronds and sisal fibre. Most of the canneries' output is consumed locally.

Marketing and Co-operative Organizations

The Bahamas Produce Exchange in Nassau handles much of the agricultural produce of the Colony. It assists the farmers in New Providence and Out Islands in disposing of their produce without glutting the market and depressing prices unnecessarily.

There are no co-operative societies in the Colony other than 17 Tomato Growers' Associations on the Islands of Eleuthera and Cat Island and 21 active Farmers' Associations situated throughout the Colony. These associations are assisted by the Ministry of Agriculture and Fisheries to the extent of advancing seed and fertilizer, etc., for the tomato and other crops. The Ministry markets the crops and deducts their advances from the proceeds of each consignor.

BARBADOS
Area, etc.
Barbados is the most easterly of the Caribbean islands. Its total area is 166 square miles.
Population
On the 31st December 1963, the estimated population was 240,341.
Principal Town
Bridgetown, the capital, is the only town. Before the 1960 census its population was estimated at about 11,304.
Geographical Features

The Island is comparatively flat, rising in a series of tablelands marked by well-defined terraces to the highest point (1,104 feet) at Mount Hillaby. The north-east corner of the Island, the Scotland area, is broken country, much eroded and rather barren. The formation of the rest of the Island is coral limestone. There are no rivers, but deep gullies which fill with water during heavy rain have cut their way through the coral terraces in many places. Indigenous forest covers about 46 acres.
Climate

The climate is extremely pleasant. North-easterly trade winds blow steadily from December to June but during the remainder of the year, the wet season, the wind moves to the south-east and is less strong, resulting in humid, hotter conditions. The average temperature is 79.8°. The rainfall
BARBADOS
is very varied : in the high central district the yearly average is 75 inches while in some of the low-lying coastal areas the average is 50 inches.
History

Uncertainty remains on some points in the early history of Barbados and differing versions have appeared from time to time. The brief account which follows is based on what is taken to be the most authentic version.

As far as is known the original inhabitants were the Arawak Indians who migrated from the mainland of South America. When they arrived is not known, but relics of their occupation down to the latter years of the sixteenth century have been discovered in various parts of the Island. By the time the English settlers came they had disappeared, partly through Carib raids from the neighbouring islands, and partly through the Spaniards who deported them to work in the mines of Hispaniola.

In spite of the belief to the contrary, there is no satisfactory evidence that the Olive Branch under Captain Catlin ever touched at Barbados when it was driven off its course from England to the River Wiapoco settlement in 1605 and fetched up at St. Lucia. The first Englishman to visit Barbados may have been Captain Simon Gordan, who claimed that he had landed some time before the settlement of 1627 and had found the Island uninhabited. He may, however, have been on a ship under the command of Sir Thomas Warner that visited sometime between 1620 and 1624 and found the Island suffering from such a severe drought that the latter preferred settlement of his colonists at St. Kitts. In 1624 or early 1625 a ship, belonging to Sir William Courteen and under the command of Captain John Powell, touched at Barbados, when en route from Pernambuco to England; some men were landed who erected a cross and on a tree nearby inscribed "James K. of E. and of this Island". On receiving Powell's information about Barbados Sir William Courteen fitted out an expedition to the Island under the command of his informant; but Powell, who carried letters of marque, captured a Spanish or Portuguese prize and returned with it to Cowes in 1626. Courteen then fitted out a second expedition and in February 1627, the William and John under the command of Captain Henry Powell—Captain John Powell's younger brother—landed 80 settlers. During 1627 and 1628 the colony grew rapidly and by the end of 1628 it was claimed that Courteen had planted 1,850 settlers. The Earl of Carlisle obtained a grant from Charles I for all the Caribbean islands and landed settlers in 1628. After his death his son leased the Island in 1647 for 21 years to Lord Willoughby of Parham who assumed the government of the Island and during the Civil War held it for the Royalists until his capitulation to the Cromwellian fleet in 1652. A Governor was appointed by the Commonwealth in place of Lord Willoughby and at the Restoration the claims of the Carlisle Patent were revived. An agreement was reached by which the Patent was surrendered to the Crown for compensation in the form of 4½% duty on exports. This duty was a continual source of complaint but was not abolished until 1838 by Act of Imperial Parliament. The importance of the agreement, however, is that it marked the end of proprietary rule. Since its settlement Barbados has been a possession of Great Britain.
Constitution
Barbados has one of the oldest constitutions in the Commonwealth and one in which conventions

play an important part. Amendments to the constitution, providing for full internal self-government came into operation on the 16th October 1961, and further amendments were made and embodied in Statutory Instrument No. 491 which was brought into operation on 9th April 1964.

The Legislature consists of the Governor, an Upper House—the Senate—consisting of 21 members appointed by the Governor, twelve of whom are appointed on the advice of the Premier and two on the advice of the Leader of the Opposition; and a Lower House—the House of Assembly—consisting of 24 members elected by universal adult suffrage. The life of both Houses is now limited to five years.

The revised Constitutional Instruments include provision for a Privy Council, a Cabinet, and the establishment of Judicial and Legal Service, Public Service and Police Service Commissions with executive powers to deal with appointments, dismissals and disciplinary control of members of the services for which they are responsible.

The Privy Council consists of the Governor and other persons (at present four, including the Minister designated Attorney-General and the Premier) appointed by Her Majesty. It advises the Governor in the exercise of the Royal Prerogative of Mercy and in the exercise of his disciplinary powers over members of the Public Service and the Police Service who may appeal to the Privy Council against disciplinary action recommended to be taken against them.
The Cabinet consists of the Premier and not less than 5 other Ministers—there are at present 7—appointed by the Governor on the advice of the Premier; it is the principal instrument of policy.

The Governor appoints as Premier the member of the House of Assembly who appears to him best able to command the support of the majority of the members of the Assembly. On the Premier's advice the Governor appoints other Ministers from among the Senators and the members of the House of Assembly. The Premier and other Ministers hold office during the Governor's pleasure.
Land Policy
The systems of land tenure are similar to those of the United Kingdom.
Taxation

Income tax is the main form of direct taxation. The tax rates range from 3 cents on every complete $ of taxable income up to $500, to 75 cents on every complete $ of taxable income beyond $24,000. Tax is not paid on incomes below $800 per annum in the case of single individuals and $1,400 in the case of married persons. A wife's earned income allowance not exceeding $300 is granted. Deductions from assessable income of $270 to $600 (varying according to the age of the child, and educational establishment at which full time instruction is being received) are allowed for children and $240 for other dependants, in the latter case not exceeding two. Deductions are also permitted in respect of life insurances paid. Medical expenses may be deducted in excess of $20 but not exceeding in the aggregate $100, in the case of a tax-payer of married status, or $50 in the case of any other person, plus $25 for each child, not exceeding two. Companies pay a flat rate of 40%. The rate for life assurance companies and building societies is the average paid by an individual with a taxable income of $4,800.
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
A non-cumulative system of Pay As You Earn is in operation.

Estate duties range from 1% on estates exceeding $4,800 and not exceeding $14,400 to 30% on estates over $1,920,000. In addition, succession duty ranges from $\frac{1}{2}\%$ between $96 and $2,400 to 2% of values exceeding $9,600 where the successor is a lineal descendant or ancestor 1% to 6% where successor is a brother, sister or their descendants, and from 2% to 10% where the successor is a stranger.
Entertainment tax is charged on certain forms of entertainment.
The other main forms of taxation are the Customs tariffs and excise duty on rum production.
Public Finance
In 1963–64 $15,915,139 was derived from Customs and Excise and $11,343,548 from Direct Taxation.
The main heads of expenditure for the same year were as follows:

At 1st April 1964 the amount of Surplus balance was $6,241,000.
Currency

The currency in circulation is a West Indian currency issued by the Board of the Commissioners of Currency, British Caribbean Territories (Eastern group) consisting of Barbados, British Guiana, the Leeward Islands, Trinidad and Tobago and the Windward Islands. Currency notes are of denominations $1, $2, $5, $10, $20 and $100. British coin is legal tender but the chief medium of circulation is the new British Caribbean coin in denominations of 50 cents, 25 cents, 10 cents, 5 cents, 2 cents, 1 cent and a $\frac{1}{2}$ cent. The West Indian $ is fixed at 4s. 2d., that is $4.80 to £1 sterling.

31.3

The aim of the plan is the promotion of the Island's economic development resulting in increased production and higher national income. Great emphasis is placed on a three-pronged drive for rapid industrial development, agricultural diversification and a bigger tourist trade. In the main, the plan aims at provision of substantial incentives to investment by way of fiscal reliefs, intensification of promotional efforts by the Development Board and the Tourist Board, the establishment of a development finance corporation to channel overseas and local capital into manufacturing industry, the expansion of the facilities for technical education and the establishment of a hotel training school, improvement of the basic communication facilities (airport, roads), the continuation of the housing programme and the encouragement of private investment in housing through mortgage insurance and the promotion of building societies, carrying out of research investigation and the exploration of the commercial feasibility of new productive possibilities, completion of the new general hospital and construction of district hospitals, and provision of increased office accommodation.
Education

Education is provided by Government at the following levels: Primary, Secondary, University, Adult and Further. Primary Education is provided in Government Primary Schools, Secondary in the Senior Departments of the Primary Schools, the Government Comprehensive (formerly Secondary Modern) Schools, the Secondary Grammar Schools maintained by Government and in some Private Independent Schools. Present policy aims at the introduction of compulsory education for all children between the ages of 5 and 14 years.

Tuition at the Primary and Comprehensive levels is free. Tuition fees were abolished for Barbadian children in the Government maintained Grammar Schools from January 1962. The Preparatory Departments of these schools were abolished at the same time. A Common Entrance Examination for children between the ages of 10 and 12 was instituted for entry to Government Grammar Schools. Improved and additional accommodation in the Primary and Comprehensive Schools is being provided under a School Building Programme by the extension and replacement of existing schools and construction of new schools where necessary.

There were, in 1963, 117 Primary Schools (31 for boys, 34 for girls and 52 co-educational, including 6 infant schools), 5 Comprehensive Schools and 10 Secondary Grammar Schools entirely maintained by Government. The number of children in the Primary Schools at the end of
BARBADOS

the year 1962–63 was 41,859 (21,214 boys, 20,645 girls), in Comprehensive Schools 5,094 (2,549 boys, 2,545 girls), in Secondary Grammar Schools 4,434 (2,609 boys and 1,825 girls). There were 117 Headteachers and 758 Assistants in the Primary Schools, 5 Headteachers and 180 Assistants in the Comprehensive Schools, 10 Headteachers and 195 Assistants in the Grammar Schools and 112 and 36 Supernumerary and Relief Teachers in the Primary and Comprehensive Schools respectively.

Erdiston Training College: with an intake of 120 students offers simultaneously one-year and two-year training courses for teachers including 30 teachers from the Leeward and Windward Islands. Teacher-training is also provided by means of Induction Classes and vacation inservice courses.

Technical and Vocational Education is provided: at the Industrial Arts Wings of Comprehensive and Grammar Schools for boys, at the Barbados Technical Institute (enrolment 553) which offers both day and evening classes from the pre-apprenticeship level to the standard of craftsmen and City and Guilds Institute of London certificate, and through a system of Apprenticeship Bursaries for school-leavers financed by Government. The Housecraft Centre specialises in Domestic Science and Home Economics training for girls.

Government awards from its own resources yearly: five Barbados Scholarships (one restricted to girls) to the University of the West Indies and Commonwealth Universities overseas, two exhibitions, three special awards (in Engineering, Agriculture and one for girls) and ten enabling Bursaries all to the University of the West Indies, as well as teachers scholarships and enabling grants to students of exhibition standard for study at University level. There is also a Higher Education Loan Fund, established in 1953, from which loans are made to students for University study abroad. A number of awards, Commonwealth scholarships for example, are also available from donor Governments and external agencies annually for University and postgraduate study abroad.

Government makes an annual subsidy towards the University of the West Indies which now has constituent colleges in Jamaica, Trinidad and Barbados. In addition Government meets the tuition fees of all Barbadians who are full-time students of the local Colleges of Arts and Science established in 1963. Codrington College, formerly a degree-granting affiliate of Durham University in England, is now a theological seminary.

During the academic year 1962–63 there were 97 Government scholars, exhibitioners, teacher trainees and bursars attending universities overseas—37 in the United Kingdom, 2 in Canada and 58 at the University of the West Indies. 35 Students were granted loans from the Higher Education Loan Fund. Of these 10 were attending universities in the United Kingdom, 17 were at the University of the West Indies, 1 at a university in the United States of America, 3 in Canada, 3 in India and 1 in Germany. In addition, there were some 20 students taking advantage of awards from external aid agencies and donor Governments for further studies abroad.

Evening classes in academic, commercial and technical subjects and in the fields of Handicraft and Home Economics are conducted under the aegis of the Barbados Evening Institute and through the Extra Mural Department of the University of the West Indies at some 27 centres throughout the island. Students enrolled in the Institute's classes during 1962–63 numbered 2,702.
Expenditure on education in the financial year April 1962—March 1963 was $5,732,396 compared with $1,974,705 in 1952–53 and $566,205 in 1942–43.
Libraries and Museums

A free Public Library (145,006 books) is maintained by Government. The Barbados Museum and Historical Society possesses collections of books, pictures, models, specimens of birds and fishes, and a collection of relics of the original Arawak inhabitants of the island.
Health

The Barbados General Hospital has accommodation for 46 paying patients and 407 non-paying patients. Admissions numbered 10,942, out patient and casualty attendances 162,770 in 1963–64. The New General Hospital, although not entirely completed received its first patients in the Ophthalmic Ward on 27th July 1964. The bed complement of this Hospital is 546 public and 87 private, making a total of 633. The Government maintains a Mental Hospital with accommodation for 321 males and 441 females, and a Leper Hospital at which there are 12 patients. For the training of midwives the Government Maternity Hospital has accommodation for 20 expectant mothers. Each of the 11 parishes has an infirmary for the care of the destitute sick and for the housing of the aged and poor. These infirmaries provide medical care and in some parishes function as district hospitals. The District Hospital at Oistins, Christ Church, provides 20 beds: 10 for maternity and 10 for ante-natal cases.

Preventive medicine is based on the Health Centre system. Three centres are operating in St. Michael, St. Peter and St. Philip. Two of these centres operate out-station clinics in various parts of the Island.
Estimated Central Government expenditure on Health Services amounted to $3,591,229 in 1963–64 compared with $206,870 in 1935–36.
Communications

An International Airport is situated at Seawell, 12 miles from Bridgetown. Air France, British Overseas Airways Corporation, British West Indian Airways, Pan American World Airways and Trans-Canada Air Lines operate frequent scheduled services connecting Barbados with the major world air routes.
There are 698 miles of road open to traffic, most of which has a bituminous surface.
Bridgetown is the only port of entry, although some loading of sugar takes place at Speightstown and oil is pumped ashore at Spring Gardens and an Esso installation on the West Coast.

The main shipping companies visiting Barbados are Harrison Line, Geest Line, Royal Netherlands Steamship Company, Compagnie Générale Transatlantique, Saguenay Shipping Ltd., Booth Line, Lamport and Holt Line, Alcoa Steamship Company, M.A.N.Z. Line, Moore McCormack Line, Nourse Line, Caribbean-Hamburg Line (formerly Three Bays Line) and Federal Shipping Service. Occasionally ships of the Linea "C" and Grimaldi Lines visit port.
Broadcasting and Television
Barbados has a television service, a wireless broadcasting service and a wired broadcasting service. The first two are operated by the Caribbean Broadcasting Corporation, a corporate body
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

set up by Order-in-Council of the Barbados Government in 1963. The wired system, which covers the whole island and had 25,412 subscribers at the 31st December 1963, is operated by Barbados Rediffusion Service Limited, a local subsidiary of the London firm of Rediffusion Limited.
Principal Occupations

Most of the population is engaged in the cultivation of the sugar crop and auxiliary occupations connected with the sugar industry. Approximate figures are: 19,500 persons engaged in agriculture, 2,000 in domestic service, 12,500 in the retail trade and 8,500 in construction work. About 1,700 persons are engaged in the fishing industry.
At the end of 1963 there were nine Trade Unions of which 3 were employers' associations with membership totalling about 340 business concerns and six workers' organizations with about 15,500 members.
Main Crops and Products
Sugar cane is the principal export crop grown on all plantations and on nearly all small holdings. In 1964 sugar production was the equivalent of 161,000 long tons.

Yams, sweet potatoes, eddoes, cassava, maize and pigeon peas comprise the main food crops which are grown in accordance with regulations issued annually. Green vegetables and other crops are cultivated on a minor scale and there is a limited production of tree crops and citrus fruit. A marketing Corporation has been established by statute for the purpose of improving the facilities for marketing in order to encourage an increase in the production of food crops, meat and dairy produce and fish.
The estimated numbers of livestock are as follows:
Cattle, 10,240; Pigs, 15,794; Sheep, 20,268; Goats, 7,828; and Poultry, 225,903.
A Central Livestock Station has been established to investigate the possibility of developing an economic system of animal husbandry, with special attention to dairying.

A Fisheries Section of the Ministry of Agriculture was established in 1944 for the investigation of more efficient methods of marine fishing and to encourage their use. Large scale mechanisation of the fishing fleet has been practically accomplished with the assistance of loans from Government. Cold Storage facilities are available for meat and for fish and a shrimp industry being developed. The estimated catch of fish during 1963 was 6,277,383 lbs.
Production of natural gas during the year ended 31st March 1964 was 117,662 million cubic feet. During 1963, 4,392.0 tons of temper, building and manurial limestone were exported.

Manufactures include the production of sugar for local consumption, rum, bread and a variety of biscuits, edible oil, aerated waters, cigarettes, soap, ice, furniture, jewellery, mattresses, lumber, ready-to-wear garments, shoes, batteries and building products.

BASUTOLAND
Area

Comprises an area of 11,716 square miles of which about one quarter in the west is lowland country varying in height above sea level from 5,000 to 6,000 feet, the remaining three quarters being highlands rising to a height of 11,425 feet in the Drakensberg Range, which forms the eastern boundary with Natal.
Population
In 1956 (the date of the last census) the population figures for Maseru township were: Africans 5,091, Europeans 590, Mixed Race 57 and one Asian.
A census of all races held in April 1956 gave the following figures for the population present in the

Territory: Africans 638,857; Europeans 1,92 Persons of Mixed Race 644; and Asians 24 The census also gave the additional figures 154,782 as an estimate of the number of Africans temporarily absent from the Colony for work and other purposes. The estimated total population of Basutoland in July 1961 was 697,000 with an additional 130,000 absent from the Territory. (By way of comparison, a census of 1875 gave a total of 469 Europeans and 127,707 Africans in Basutoland.)
Principal Towns
The capital is Maseru, which has an estimated population of about 10,000. The other principal towns are: Butha Buthe, Leribe, Mafeteng, Mokhotlong, Qachas Nek, Mohale's Hoek and Quthing.
Geographical Features

The Colony of Basutoland is an enclave within the Republic of South Africa, its boundaries running with those of Natal to the east, the Cape Province to the south and the Orange Free State to the north and west. It lies between latitudes $28^{\circ} 35'$ and $30^{\circ} 40'$ south and longitudes $27^{\circ} 00'$ and $29^{\circ} 30'$ east. The mountain ranges run from north to south and those in the central area, named the Maluti, are spurs of the main Drakensberg which they join in the north, forming a high plateau varying in height from 9,000 to 10,500 feet. In this area two of the largest rivers in the Republic, the Orange and the Tugela, and tributaries of the Caledon, have their source.
Climate

Rainfall is variable and averages approximately 29 inches a year over the greater part of the country. Most of it falls between October and April, but there is normally no month which has less than half an inch. Unfortunately, river discharge statistics show that most of this water is lost to Basutoland in the form of run-off. The deciding factors from an agricultural point of view are whether the rain comes in steady soaking showers at intervals suited to the growth of the staple crops or whether it comes in the form of short and heavy storms, running to waste and eroding the soil. Serious droughts, like the one of 1948–49 are rare and there are a large number of perennial streams, though not so many as existed some years ago. In the lowlands the temperatures vary from a maximum of $90^{\circ}$ or more in summer to a minimum that rarely drops below $20^{\circ}$ in winter: in the highlands the range is much wider and temperatures below zero are common.
History

The Basotho as a nation did not come into prominence until 1818 when Moshesh, a minor chief of the Bakoena tribe in Northern Basutoland, gathered together the remnants of the various clans that had been scattered by the raids of the Zulu and Matebele.

The new nation went through a period of struggle, first with the emigrant Boers of the Great Trek, then with the British. From 1856 until 1868 there was a series of wars with the Orange Free State burghers, Moshoeshoe lost a large portion of his country and repeatedly sought the protection of the British Government.
In 1868 the Basotho became British subjects and the boundaries of the Territory were defined by the Cape of Good Hope Notice of 13th May 1870.
BASUTOLAND

The country remained in a very unsettled condition until it was annexed to the Cape Colony in 1871. The people never took kindly to the Cape Colonial rule and in 1880, after various disturbances, the Governor of the Cape extended to Basutoland the provisions of the Cape Peace Preservation Act of 1878, which included a clause for the general disarmament of the Basotho. They refused to accept the terms, and, after a war lasting nearly a year, the Act was repealed, several chiefs would not submit, and it was decided to hand over the administration to the Imperial Government. This was effected by Order in Council of 2nd February 1884.
Constitution

Before the introduction of constitutional reforms in March 1960 Basutoland was governed under a loose-knit system by which the British Administration combined with an hereditary Chieftainship. The Basotho owe allegiance to a single Paramount Chief under whom there is a hierarchy of Chiefs and Headmen. These Chiefs and Headmen are the authorities with whom the ordinary people deal in their daily affairs, and are responsible for the day to day administration of the Territory.
The Head of the Administration is the Resident Commissioner.

In 1910 an advisory body known as the Basutoland Council consisting of the Resident Commissioner as President, the Paramount Chief as Chief Councillor and 99 Basotho nominated members, was constituted by Proclamation. The Council evolved gradually and an elected element, 42 in number, was introduced in 1957.
In 1943 District Councils, consisting in the main of nominated members and a small number of elected members, were established as advisory and consultative bodies.

In September 1955 the Basutoland National Council passed a motion requesting "that the Basutoland Council be given power to make laws in all internal matters, such laws to be confirmed by the Paramount Chief". In May 1956 the Secretary of State invited the Council to submit detailed recommendations for an advance in constitutional status. Agreement on a new constitution was reached at the end of 1958 and this was brought into operation in March 1960.
The new constitution provided for an Executive Council which is advisory to both the Resident Commissioner and the Paramount Chief, and a Legislative Council known as the Basutoland National Council.

The Executive Council consists of the Resident Commissioner, who acts as Chairman, the Government Secretary, Finance Secretary, Assistant Attorney-General, three persons chosen by the Basutoland National Council from among their own number and one person nominated by the Paramount Chief.

The Basutoland National Council consists of 80 members, divided equally between elected and non-elected members; the former being elected from among the members of District Councils by the District Councils sitting as electoral colleges for that purpose. The non-elected element is made up of the three official members of the Executive Council (excluding the Chairman), the Commissioner for Local Government, the 22 Principal and Ward Chiefs and 14 nominees of the Paramount Chief. The Council has power to legislate for all persons in Basutoland in respect of all matters, save the following, which are the Resident Commissioner's matters:

External Affairs and Defence; Internal Security; Currency, Public Loans, Customs and Excise; Copyright, Patents, Trade Marks and Designs; Posts (including Post Office Savings Bank), Telegrams, Telephones, Broadcasting and Television; the recruitment, appointment, conditions of service, promotion, discipline and retirement (including pensions) of officers in the Public Service.
In respect of legislation within the Resident Commissioner's field, the Basutoland National Council continue to act as a consultative body and all matters will be referred to it.
National elections under the constitution were held in January 1960 to elect the 162 members of the nine District Councils. The results were:

In September 1961 the Basutoland National Council passed a resolution inviting the Paramount Chief to appoint a Constitutional Commission to review the working of the Constitution and to formulate proposals for constitutional advance.

The Constitutional Commission, which was appointed early in 1962 and which consisted of representatives of the main political parties and of the Chieftainship, submitted a report to the Paramount Chief on 3rd October 1963.

In April/May 1964 there was a further Constitutional Conference and in the report Cmnd. 2371 following this conference it was stated that if at any time not earlier than one year after the new elections the people of Basutoland should ask for independence, the British Government would seek to give effect to their wishes as soon as possible. The British Government would therefore from now on base their policy on the firm expectation that the request for independence would be confirmed one year after the elections and take effect as soon as possible thereafter. The Secretary of State expressed the opinion that a preparatory period of one year might well prove insufficient. Nevertheless the British Government did not wish to withhold independence longer than the people of Basutoland considered necessary and were therefore disposed to approve a constitution for independence on the general lines recommended in the Constitutional Commission's report. To this end arrangements are being made forthwith to accelerate the localisation of the public service within the Territory. In the period before independence the British Government will retain responsibility for defence, external affairs, internal security and the terms and conditions of service of public officers. The next Basutoland elections are scheduled to be held in March/April 1965.

The new constitution will give Basutoland a Legislative Council elected by universal adult suffrage and a Senate consisting of the twenty-two Principal Chiefs and eleven other persons nominated by the Paramount Chief.

The National Assembly will consist of 60 members, each of whom will represent a single member constituency. The Paramount Chief will be Her Majesty's Representative and a new office of British Government Representative will be established.

BASUTOLAND
Area

Comprises an area of 11,716 square miles of which about one quarter in the west is lowland country varying in height above sea level from 5,000 to 6,000 feet, the remaining three quarters being highlands rising to a height of 11,425 feet in the Drakensberg Range, which forms the eastern boundary with Natal.
Population
In 1956 (the date of the last census) the population figures for Maseru township were: Africans 5,091, Europeans 590, Mixed Race 57 and one Asian.
A census of all races held in April 1956 gave the following figures for the population present in the

Territory: Africans 638,857; Europeans 1,926; Persons of Mixed Race 644; and Asians 247. The census also gave the additional figures of 154,782 as an estimate of the number of Africans temporarily absent from the Colony for work and other purposes. The estimated total population of Basutoland in July 1961 was 697,000 with an additional 130,000 absent from the Territory. (By way of comparison, a census of 1875 gave a total of 469 Europeans and 127,707 Africans in Basutoland.)
Principal Towns
The capital is Maseru, which has an estimated population of about 10,000. The other principal towns are: Butha Buthe, Leribe, Mafeteng, Mokhotlong, Qachas Nek, Mohale's Hoek and Quthing.
Geographical Features

The Colony of Basutoland is an enclave within the Republic of South Africa, its boundaries running with those of Natal to the east, the Cape Province to the south and the Orange Free State to the north and west. It lies between latitudes $28^{\circ} 35'$ and $30^{\circ} 40'$ south and longitudes $27^{\circ} 00'$ and $29^{\circ} 30'$ east. The mountain ranges run from north to south and those in the central area, named the Maluti, are spurs of the main Drakensberg which they join in the north, forming a high plateau varying in height from 9,000 to 10,500 feet. In this area two of the largest rivers in the Republic, the Orange and the Tugela, and tributaries of the Caledon, have their source.
Climate

Rainfall is variable and averages approximately 29 inches a year over the greater part of the country. Most of it falls between October and April, but there is normally no month which has less than half an inch. Unfortunately, river discharge statistics show that most of this water is lost to Basutoland in the form of run-off. The deciding factors from an agricultural point of view are whether the rain comes in steady soaking showers at intervals suited to the growth of the staple crops or whether it comes in the form of short and heavy storms, running to waste and eroding the soil. Serious droughts, like the one of 1948–49 are rare and there are a large number of perennial streams, though not so many as existed some years ago. In the lowlands the temperatures vary from a maximum of $90^{\circ}$ or more in summer to a minimum that rarely drops below $20^{\circ}$ in winter: in the highlands the range is much wider and temperatures below zero are common.
History

The Basotho as a nation did not come into prominence until 1818 when Moshesh, a minor chief of the Bakoena tribe in Northern Basutoland, gathered together the remnants of the various clans that had been scattered by the raids of the Zulu and Matebele.

The new nation went through a period of struggle, first with the emigrant Boers of the Great Trek, then with the British. From 1856 until 1868 there was a series of wars with the Orange Free State burghers, Moshoeshoe lost a large portion of his country and repeatedly sought the protection of the British Government.
In 1868 the Basotho became British subjects and the boundaries of the Territory were defined by the Cape of Good Hope Notice of 13th May 1870.

THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST
Majesty through the Secretary of State, with the powers of a Governor. The Government Secretary became the Chief Secretary and the Assistant Attorney General became the Attorney General.
Taxation
The main forms of direct taxation are Basuto tax, income tax and poll tax (payable by all male adult persons who do not pay Basuto tax).

The principal sources of indirect revenue are customs and excise duties at varying rates. Basutoland is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades and businesses. There are also death duties, stamp duty and a wool and mohair export duty.
Public Finance
Excluding Colonial Development and Welfare funds.

As revenue was expected to be substantially below expenditure and the Territory had no central balance the United Kingdom Government approved grant-in-aid of £1,694,500 for 1964–65.
Currency

The currency is the same as in the Republic of South Africa. During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from Sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 Sterling = R.2; 1 shilling = 10 cents).
Development Plans

Under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act 1959 Basutoland received an allocation of £1.2m. in grants and exchequer loans towards development over the period 1959–64. The programme provided for both social and economic projects, the main headings being:

£
Soil conservation and agriculture 400,000
Medical services .. .. 143,000
Education .. .. 119,000
Roads and airfields .. .. 104,000
Water and electricity supplies .. 95,000
A further allocation of £418,000 was made in 1961 for development to March 1963. This was used principally towards Education, Medical, Agriculture and Veterinary services.

A further allocation of £1.2m. was approved in September 1963, and with the balances available under the earlier allocations, this provides some £1.703m. for development in the period ending March 1966, again being used principally for agricultural, medical, educational and public works services.
Education

African education is largely in the hands of three missions (Paris Evangelical, Roman Catholic and Anglican), under the direction of the Education Department. Grants-in-aid are paid by Government to the three missions to cover the salaries of all teachers on the approved grant-in-aid list. In many aided schools of the Roman Catholic mission, assistance is given by unaided teachers, particularly Sisters. Small grants are paid from territorial funds to improve the equipment in primary schools. Estimated expenditure on grants-in-aid during financial year 1963–64 is £536,750.

There are 1,102 schools and institutions in the Territory of which all but 13 are under mission control, and of these mission schools 985 are either wholly or partly aided. The total enrolment in primary schools is 151,719, with 2,672 teachers of whom 1,205 have no professional certificate. In secondary schools the enrolment is 2,209, with 104 teachers all with professional training. The enrolment in the teacher training schools is 584, in schools offering a technical course 171 and in housecraft schools 372.

The University of Basutoland, Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland formed by Royal Charter, formally came into existence on the 1st January 1964, and is the independent successor to the Roman Catholic Pius XII College at Roma. It will award its own degrees, and is intended by 1967 to have some 300 students primarily, but not exclusively, from Basutoland, Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland. Students are to be enrolled regardless of race, sex, nationality or religion. The immediate aim of the University is to meet the needs of the Territory and of the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland for qualified administrators in the Civil Service and the Judiciary, and in the trading profession, and to produce graduates qualified in Agriculture, Law, Economics and Commerce.
Tuition in primary schools is free, and bursaries are available for the most deserving cases at all stages of secondary, teacher training and University work.
The number of known Basotho students abroad on full time courses of a year's duration or more at the end of 1963 was 87, an increase of some 30 on the figure for the end of 1962.
Health

The Government Medical staff of the Territory consists of a Director of Health, 22 Medical Officers, one Medical Officer of Health, one Medical Officer for Mental Health and one Surgical Specialist. There are nine Government hospitals, staffed by European Nursing Sisters with subordinate African staff. There is accommodation for 664 patients in the Government hospitals. A 230 bed Government hospital in Maseru, which with the gracious consent of Her Majesty has been named the Queen Elizabeth II Hospital, was officially opened on the 25th July 1957. There are six mission hospitals subsidized by the Government, with a total accommodation of 467 beds. Health centres in mountain dispensaries provide out-patient medical facilities, and, where a trained African nurse is a member of the staff, maternity services to people living in remote areas. During 1962 19,480 patients were admitted to Government hospitals and 7,366 to mission hospitals. During the period 1937 to 1962 the outpatients at both Government and Mission dispensaries and clinics increased from 110,807 to 738,499. The Leper Settlement, four miles out of Maseru, had 326 patients at the end of 1962.
BASUTOLAND

The principal diseases are venereal disease, chronic rheumatism, malnutrition, infections of the respiratory tract and dyspepsia. The heaviest toll of lives in children is due to tuberculosis, malnutrition, diphtheria, whooping-cough and gastro-enteritis. The incidence of nutritional and deficiency diseases is comparatively high and is allied to maize being the staple food. Typhus, plague and smallpox occur only rarely, and then in sporadic form, while diphtheria, typhoid fever, measles and whooping cough are endemic.
Communications

The Territory is linked with the railway system of the Republic of South Africa by a short branch-line from Maseru, the capital, to Marseilles on the Bloemfontein-Natal main line. One mile of this line is in Basutoland. The South African Railway runs near the boundaries of the Territory and goods are transported by road to the nearest railway station across the border.

The Road Motor Transport Department of the South African Railways runs regular services between Fouriesburg and Butha-Buthe, Zastron and Quthing, Zastron and Mohale's Hock, and Quthing and Sebapala, and within the Territory in the lowland areas there are a number of bus services owned and operated by Basotho.

The greater part of Basutoland is very mountainous and in this area all transport is by pack animals. A new mountain motor road running eastwards from Maseru into the mountains has been constructed in order to open up the interior and facilitate its development and administration. The road has reached a point 80 miles from Maseru.

On the western side of Basutoland a fairly good road system has been developed in the strip of agricultural country running from north to south. The main roads traverse the whole of this area, with branches leading west to the principal points of exit. From the main roads a number of feeder-roads lead towards the foothills.
Principal Occupations

Basutoland is a pastoral country without factories or industrial undertakings, and since the produce of the land is insufficient to support the total population, many able-bodied Basotho leave the Territory each year to take up employment in the Republic of South Africa. The Government protects labourers by forbidding recruitment for any mine or other concern where the conditions of work are not good. All labourers recruited by agents in Basutoland for work outside the Territory have to be attested before an official and the contract must be according to Government Regulations.
Main Crops and Products

So far there has been little evidence of any exploitable mineral deposits in the Territory, but recent prospecting for diamonds has shown encouraging results. For the present, agriculture and animal husbandry are Basutoland's main sources of wealth. The chief exports at present are wheat, wool and mohair. It is estimated that 1,000,000 acres are under cultivation.

The soils in the mountain areas are of basaltic origin, while those in the lowlands are derived mainly from underlying cave sandstone. In the lowlands the soil has been cropped continuously for upwards of 80 years. Due to the absence of fuel, practically all cattle manure is burnt, so that little or no organic matter is returned to the land.

Thus with increasing population, both human and livestock, excessive demands have been made on the soil, which has lost its structure and become subject to serious erosion. The soils in the mountains which have been brought into cultivation comparatively recently are rich though shallow. Controlled grazing has brought back a grass cover and has effectively halted the loss of soil by storm water run off in the mountain areas. In 1961 some 1,252,000 trees were planted as part of soil conservation measures.
In 1962 there were 376,740 head of cattle and 1,227,386 sheep in the Territory. Wool, followed by mohair, hides and skins, continue to be the main source of cash income from the animal population.
Trade

Basutoland has no industries and imports the consumer goods and capital items it needs as well as a certain amount of agricultural produce and livestock. Exports consist almost entirely of agricultural commodities and livestock, the main exports being wool and mohair, followed by wheat, sorghum and cattle. The value of imports usually exceeds the value of exports but the adverse balance is partly offset by the export of labour to the Republic of South Africa.

THE BECHUANALAND PROTECTORATE
Area

The Protectorate has not been surveyed as a whole, but its area is estimated at 222,000 square miles. The headquarters of the Protectorate Administration is at Gaberones, the new capital, to which the administration moved from Mafeking in February 1965.
Population
The census taken in 1956 showed a population of 3,173 Europeans, 676 Coloured, 248 Asians and 316,578 Africans. The provisional figure for 1964 Census of total population is 540,401.

The population is not evenly distributed, the bulk of the people living in the eastern part of the Protectorate with one fairly large tribe occupying the north-western corner. The central, western and southern areas of the territory, consisting for the most part of the Kalahari Desert, are extremely sparsely inhabited.
The Bechuana tribes tend to live in large villages or towns. About one-half of the population lives in towns of more than 1,000 inhabitants.
Principal Towns
Francistown, Gaberones, Kanye, Lobatsi, Maun, Molepolole, Mochudi and Serowe.
Geographical Features
The Bechuanaland Protectorate is bounded on the south by the Province of the Cape of Good
BECHUANALAND

Hope, on the south-east by the Transvaal, on the north-east by Southern Rhodesia, on the north by the Caprivi Zipfel and on the west by South West Africa. It lies between latitudes $18^{\circ}$ and $37^{\circ}$ south and longitudes $20^{\circ}$ and $29^{\circ} 50'$ east. The Kalahari Desert extends over most of the Protectorate west and south of Ngamiland as far as latitude $27^{\circ}$ S. This is not a desert in the commonly accepted sense of the word, but consists of a vast expanse of undulating sand-belts with outcrops of limestone. In these limestone belts water is sometimes found at depths from 30 to 100 feet and it is possible that under the deep overburden of sand there may exist untapped reservoirs. The northern and south-western corners of the Kalahari have the least vegetation and most sand dunes, but elsewhere the grasses are excellent, and this is the natural habitat of the aboriginal Bushmen. Large areas are wooded like parklands with camel-thorn and other indigenous trees.

In the north-western part of the Protectorate the Okovango River, flowing inland from Angola, widens into a delta from which flood-waters, in seasons of heavy rainfall, flow as far south-east as the Makarikari salt-lake and south and south-west into Lake Ngami. The Chobe River, also flowing from Angola, inundates a large area in the north before flowing into the Zambesi 60 miles west of the Victoria Falls.
The mean altitude of the Protectorate is 3,300 feet and in the eastern portion occasional elevations, e.g. at Kanye, Serowe and Ghanzi, reach 4,000 to 5,000 feet.
The Protectorate as a whole is a natural game-reserve for most species of the fauna of Africa and Government policy aims at their preservation.
Climate
The climate is sub-tropical (latitude $22^{\circ}$ S. passes through the centre of the Protectorate) but varies with latitude and altitude.
The average annual rainfall is 18 inches, varying from 27 inches in the north to 9 inches or less in the western Kalahari.

In the higher parts, days are pleasantly warm and the nights cold during the winter, but the summer is universally hot. In August seasonal winds blow from the west coast and, desiccated by the Kalahari, carry sand and dust across the whole country.
The atmosphere throughout the year is very dry and this helps somewhat to mitigate the high temperature.
History

In 1884 the British Government appointed a Deputy Commissioner to the Bechuana tribes and in 1885 the whole of the Bechuanaland was, with the concurrence of Khama and other principal chiefs, proclaimed to be under the protection of Her Majesty The Queen. The southern part of the territory, which included Mafeking, Vryburg and Kuruman, was at the same time constituted a Crown Colony with the title British Bechuanaland. In 1885 British Bechuanaland was annexed to Cape Colony, subsequently the Cape Province of the Republic of South Africa. The northern part, known as the Bechuanaland Protectorate, has continued to be administered by the Government of the United Kingdom.
The administration of the Bechuanaland Protectorate is governed by various Orders in Council and Proclamations, of which the most important

is the Order in Council of Her Majesty Queen Victoria dated 9th May 1891. That Order empowered the then High Commissioner for South Africa to exercise on Her Majesty's behalf all the powers and jurisdiction which Her Majesty had or might have within the Protectorate and to that end authorized him to take or cause to be taken all such measures as he might think expedient, subject to such instructions as he might from time to time receive from Her Majesty or through a Secretary of State. Other provisions of the Order empowered the High Commissioner to appoint administrative and judicial officers and to assign their functions to them subject to the preservation of his own powers in their entirety and to provide, by proclamation from time to time, for the administration of justice, the raising of revenue and the peace, order and good government of all persons within the Protectorate, including the prohibition and punishment of acts tending to disturb the public peace.

In issuing these proclamations the High Commissioner was instructed by the Order in Council to respect any Native laws and customs by which the civil relations of any Native chiefs, tribes or population under Her Majesty's protection were at that time (viz., in May 1891) regulated, except in so far as the same might be incompatible with the due exercise of Her Majesty's power and jurisdiction or which were repugnant to humanity.

The Order required the High Commissioner to publish his proclamations in the Gazette and reserved to Her Majesty the right to disallow any such proclamation. The Order also provided that, subject to any proclamation lawfully issued by the High Commissioner, any jurisdiction exercisable otherwise than under this Order in Council of 1891, whether by virtue of any Statute or Order in Council, or of any treaty, or otherwise, should remain in full force.
Her Majesty reserved the power to revoke, alter, add to or amend this Order at any time.
Constitution

The Protectorate is administered by Her Majesty's Commissioner, and is divided for administrative purposes into 12 districts under District Commissioners who are assisted in the maintenance of law and order by a force of police.
The territory comprises Crown lands, blocks of European farms and tribal territories.

In the African areas the method of administration is that generally known as "indirect rule". Tribal administrations were formally established and the powers and rights of African authorities were defined in the African Administration and African Courts Proclamations of 1943 (Caps. 56 and 5 of the Laws, Cap. 56 having now been replaced by Proc. No. 24 of 1956) which replace the original Proclamations 74 and 75 of 1934. In 1938 Tribal Treasuries were successfully established in all Tribal Territories. Government pays 50% of each Tribal Administration's collection of African tax to the tribal treasury concerned; other sources of revenue are a graded tax and stand-rents. The Chiefs and Finance Committees, under the guidance of District Commissioners, are responsible for the preparation of estimates and general management of their treasuries.

On 28th July 1960 the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations announced his approval of proposals for a new constitution in the Protectorate. These proposals were promulgated by the Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution)
THE COLONIAL OFFICE LIST

Order in Council, 1960, and provided for a Legislative Council with power to legislate for the Protectorate, subject to certain reserve powers remaining with the High Commissioner. The Legislative Council had an elected unofficial majority; there were 21 elected members, of whom 10 were European, 10 African and one Asian; there were also 10 Government officials and four nominated unofficial members. The European elected members were elected by European voters, subject to certain qualifications of age, residence and property or income; the Asian elected member was elected by Asian voters similarly qualified. The African elected members were elected by an African Council consisting of Chiefs of the eight principal tribes, 32 members elected by the tribal or district councils together with the official and appointed members. The Executive Council consisted of six officials and four unofficials two of whom were European and two African.

The Bechuanaland Protectorate (Constitution) (Amendment) Order 1963 created, in place of the former post of Resident Commissioner, the post of Her Majesty's Commissioner equivalent in status to that of a Governor. It also empowered the Commissioner to assent to legislation and made him directly responsible to the Secretary of State. The post of High Commissioner for Basutoland, the Bechuanaland Protectorate and Swaziland was abolished on the 1st August 1964.

On the 2nd June 1964 the Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations and for the Colonies announced the British Government's general agreement with the unanimous proposals for the revision of the constitution formulated, during the second half of 1963, at discussions initiated by the then Resident Commissioner in which representatives of all political parties and other important groups in Bechuanaland participated. The proposals provide for a Cabinet presided over by Her Majesty's Commissioner and consisting of the Prime Minister, Deputy Prime Minister, up to five other Ministers drawn from the Legislative Assembly and the Financial Secretary as ex officio Minister of Finance. The Legislative Assembly is to consist of a Speaker, 31 elected members, four special members elected by the elected members, the Attorney-General (who may speak but not vote) and the Financial Secretary (for so long as he is ex officio Minister of Finance). A single voters' roll, based on universal adult suffrage, provides for the election of the 31 members from one-member constituencies. There is to be a House of Chiefs, consisting of the Chiefs of the 8 principal tribes and 4 other members elected by and from the sub-chiefs in the Chobe, Francistown, Ghanzi and Kgalagadi Districts with the special function of considering draft Bills affecting tribal matters as well as having the right to make representations to Government on any issue affecting the interests of the Tribes. Her Majesty's Commissioner will act in accordance with the advice of the Cabinet subject to his special responsibility for external affairs, defence and security and the public service, and the retention of general reserve executive and legislative powers. A code of fundamental human rights, enforceable by the High Court, is also contained in the constitution. Elections under the new constitution are planned for the 1st March 1965.
Taxation
The main forms of direct taxation are African tax and African graded tax, income tax, personal tax and cattle export tax. The principal sources

of indirect taxation are customs and excise duties at varying rates. The Bechuanaland Protectorate is in a customs union with the Republic of South Africa and receives a fixed percentage of the gross revenue collected on all imports into the Republic. Licensing exists for many trades, businesses and professions. There are also death duties.

During February 1961 the Territory followed the South African change from sterling to a decimalized system of Rands and Cents (£1 sterling = R. 2; 1 shilling = 10 cents). The Standard Bank of South Africa and Barclays Bank (D.C.O.) each have branches at Francistown, Lobatsi, Mahalapye and Gaberones.

This, together with a supplementary allocation of £560,000 made earlier in 1963 under the Colonial Development and Welfare Act, 1959, and the unspent balance of previous allocations, gave the Protectorate approximately £2,716,000 towards development over the period 1963-66.
